results.question: How does the Tsallis-Pareto distribution handle high-pT tails compared to standard Boltzmann-Juttner?
results.answer_preview:
> "The identified particle spectra at RHIC and LHC do not follow Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution due to the possible QCD contributions at high-pT." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Introduction [[1]]()

> "To describe the complete transverse spectra of identified particles, one has to account for the power-law contribution at high-pT  and this empirically takes care for the possible QCD contributions."
results.answer:
> "The identified particle spectra at RHIC and LHC do not follow Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution due to the possible QCD contributions at high-pT." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Introduction [[1]]()

> "To describe the complete transverse spectra of identified particles, one has to account for the power-law contribution at high-pT  and this empirically takes care for the possible QCD contributions." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Introduction [[1]]()

> "A thermodynamically consistent Tsallis non-extensive distribution function at mid-rapidity is given as follows , f(mT) = Cq [ 1 + (q − 1) mT T ]− 1 q−1 ." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Introduction [[1]]()

> "The resultant expression acts as an exponential and a power-law function for small and large pT, respectively." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Introduction [[1]]() (referring to the Hagedorn function which bridges the behaviors, but the text explicitly links Tsallis to the power-law necessity).

> "The Tsallis non-extensive distribution function accounts for the power-law contribution at high-pT and this could be understood as empirically taking of QCD contributions." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Section II.A [[3]]()

> "It is found that the Tsallis distribution generally leads to a better description for the complete transverse momentum spectra whereas the BGBW model explains the bulk part of the system." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Abstract [[3]]()

> "The BGBW model is quite good in explaining the bulk part of the system, however it fails at low-pT which could possibly be due to the decays of hadronic resonances." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Introduction [[3]]() (Note: While this quote mentions low-pT failure, the context of the paper and the previous abstract quote clarify that BGBW fails to capture the high-pT tail which requires the power-law component inherent in Tsallis).

> "In the limit of q → 1, Tsallis distribution (Eq. 3) reduces to the standard Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution (Eq. 1)." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Introduction [[1]]()

results.question: Does the manuscript compare the boson probability distribution model against a Tsallis or Blast-Wave baseline? If so, what is the stated motivation?
results.answer_preview:
> "As an alternative description, we compare the fits using the Tsallis distribution with the BGBW model." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Section II.B [[1]]()

> "As an alternative, perhaps more standard description, we have used a blast-wave model based on collective flow in small systems." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Section I [[2]]()

> "The use of Tsallis non-extensive statistics incorporates a p
results.answer:
> "As an alternative description, we compare the fits using the Tsallis distribution with the BGBW model." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Section II.B [[1]]()

> "As an alternative, perhaps more standard description, we have used a blast-wave model based on collective flow in small systems." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Section I [[2]]()

> "The use of Tsallis non-extensive statistics incorporates a power-law distribution, which has a dominant contribution at higher collision energies, making a better description of the transverse momentum spectra of charged particles and identified particles up to very high values of pT... This description can be understood as containing temperature fluctuations in the Boltzmann-Gibbs sense either on an event-by-event basis or within the same event." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Section I [[2]]()

> "The BGBW model is quite good in explaining the bulk part of the system, however it fails at low-pT which could possibly be due to the decays of hadronic resonances." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Section I [[2]]()

> "It is found that the Tsallis distribution generally leads to a better description for the complete transverse momentum spectra whereas the BGBW model explains the bulk part of the system." — Rath_2020_1908.04208.pdf, Abstract [[2]]()

> "The identified particle spectra at RHIC and LHC do not follow Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution due to the possible QCD contributions at high-pT. But the low-pT-region at high-multiplicity classes in pp-collision is explained by incorporating the radial flow (β) into Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution function, which is known as Boltzmann-Gibbs Blast Wave (BGBW) model." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Section I [[3]]()

> "To describe the complete transverse spectra of identified particles, one has to account for the power-law contribution at high-pT and this empirically takes care for the possible QCD contributions... This necessitates to use non-extensive Tsallis statistics for the description of the pT-spectra in high-energy hadronic and nuclear collisions." — Khuntia_2019_1808.02383.pdf, Section I [[3]]()
